source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
State: Social Credit, Surveillance and the CCP’s Plan for China’ (2017) 17(11) China Brief, online:
    https://jamestown.org/program/managing-the-state-social-credit-surveillance-and-the-ccps-plan-
    for-china/.
118
    The Sesame Credit Score has become a status symbol and can for example aﬀect the likeliness of
    getting a date through the online dating platform Baihe. A high Sesame Score can enable a
    Chinese citizen to use the fast-track application for the Schengen visa. See Botsman (n 22).
119
    See Creemers (n 49) 99.
120
    See European Union Chamber of Commerce in China (n 13) 32 and 34; See ‘Planning Outline for the
    Construction of a Social Credit System (2014–2020)’ (n 10).
121
    See Shaﬀer (n 2) 237.
122
    Sebastian Heilmann, ‘19. Parteitag der KP China: Die Digitalisierung spielt der Kommunistischen
    Partei in die Hände’ (2017) Mercator Institute for China Studies, online: www.merics.org/de/china-
    ﬂash/19-parteitag-der-kp-china-die-digitalisierung-spielt-der-kommunistischen-partei-die.
123
    See Shaﬀer (n 2) 249–50, 254–5.
124
    Ibid, 237.
 312         S. JAKOB

classiﬁed China as a strategic partner but also as a systemic rival.125 States
that have competing political and value systems to the Chinese one might
be less willing to be directly inﬂuenced by CSCS but the construction and
ﬂow of norms can be much more subtle in a globalised and interdependent
economy. The network of Western corporations across the world has been
securing Western inﬂuence for centuries,126 and has made the transnational
political project one that mainly serves capitalist interests. Now, this corpor-
ate network and capitalist common sense can be used by the CCP to foster its
own interests.
   Practical change within the business realm can currently be perceived in
censorship outside of China—while censorship is ‘still often ineﬀective in the
form of open threats to media outlets, [it] works well with companies
seeking business in China’.127 Once co-optation has been established and
has strengthened the dependency of an economy or a national elite, coercion
or the possibility to use coercion becomes more eﬀective. This is thus a possible
route for a transnational legal process that can lead to practical change, giving
the CCP more weight in the construction and ﬂow of norms. In addition, the
co-optation of inﬂuential individuals and corporate players further increases
the Party’s ability to inﬂuence the discourse outside China’s borders. Control
over discourse and language is not only perceived as a soft power instrument
but as a security issue by the CCP.128 In the conception of transnational law as a
political project, this is not farfetched: The CSCS harvests the economic power
of the PRC and inﬂuences the discourse and thereby knowledge structures and
conceptions of right. Consequently, this results in an increasingly authoritative
claim of power in the global political economy.
